title: Psychology: Key Experiments & Figures
description: Famous psychologists, experiments, and psychological concepts

cards.front: Ivan Pavlov — what is he known for?
cards.back: Classical conditioning (training dogs to salivate at the sound of a bell)

cards.front: Who discovered classical conditioning by experimenting with dogs and bells?
cards.back: Ivan Pavlov

cards.front: What is classical conditioning?
cards.back: A learning process in which a neutral stimulus becomes associated with a meaningful stimulus, eventually triggering the same response

cards.front: B.F. Skinner — what is he known for?
cards.back: Operant conditioning and the Skinner box (reinforcement and punishment shape behavior)

cards.front: Who developed the theory of operant conditioning and invented the 'Skinner box'?
cards.back: B.F. Skinner

cards.front: What is operant conditioning?
cards.back: A learning process in which behavior is strengthened or weakened by reinforcement or punishment

cards.front: What is a Skinner box?
cards.back: An operant conditioning chamber used to study animal behavior through reinforcement and punishment

cards.front: Sigmund Freud — what is he known for?
cards.back: Psychoanalysis and the theory of id, ego, and superego

cards.front: Who founded psychoanalysis and proposed the id, ego, and superego?
cards.back: Sigmund Freud

cards.front: What are Freud's three parts of the psyche?
cards.back: Id (primal desires), ego (rational mediator), and superego (moral conscience)

cards.front: What is psychoanalysis?
cards.back: A therapeutic approach founded by Freud that explores unconscious thoughts and childhood experiences to understand behavior

cards.front: Carl Jung — what is he known for?
cards.back: Archetypes, the collective unconscious, and analytical psychology

cards.front: Who proposed the theory of the collective unconscious and archetypes?
cards.back: Carl Jung

cards.front: What is the collective unconscious?
cards.back: Jung's idea that all humans share a set of inherited memories and universal symbols (archetypes)

cards.front: Stanley Milgram — what is he known for?
cards.back: The Milgram obedience experiment (1961), which showed people would administer what they believed were dangerous electric shocks when ordered by an authority figure

cards.front: Who conducted the famous 1961 experiment on obedience to authority using fake electric shocks?
cards.back: Stanley Milgram

cards.front: What did the Milgram experiment demonstrate?
cards.back: That ordinary people will obey authority figures even when asked to do things that conflict with their personal morals

cards.front: Philip Zimbardo — what is he known for?
cards.back: The Stanford prison experiment (1971), which studied how assigned roles affect behavior

cards.front: Who conducted the Stanford prison experiment in 1971?
cards.back: Philip Zimbardo

cards.front: What happened in the Stanford prison experiment?
cards.back: College students assigned as 'guards' became abusive and 'prisoners' became submissive; the study was ended early after only 6 days

cards.front: Jean Piaget — what is he known for?
cards.back: The four stages of cognitive development in children

cards.front: Who proposed four stages of cognitive development in children?
cards.back: Jean Piaget

cards.front: What are Piaget's four stages of cognitive development (in order)?
cards.back: Sensorimotor (0–2), Preoperational (2–7), Concrete Operational (7–11), Formal Operational (11+)

cards.front: Abraham Maslow — what is he known for?
cards.back: The hierarchy of needs (a five-level pyramid of human motivation)

cards.front: Who created the hierarchy of needs?
cards.back: Abraham Maslow

cards.front: What are the five levels of Maslow's hierarchy of needs (bottom to top)?
cards.back: Physiological, Safety, Love/Belonging, Esteem, Self-Actualization

cards.front: Erik Erikson — what is he known for?
cards.back: Eight psychosocial stages of development spanning the entire lifespan

cards.front: Who proposed eight psychosocial stages of development from infancy to old age?
cards.back: Erik Erikson

cards.front: John B. Watson — what is he known for?
cards.back: Founding behaviorism and the Little Albert experiment

cards.front: Who is considered the father of behaviorism and conducted the Little Albert experiment?
cards.back: John B. Watson

cards.front: What was the Little Albert experiment?
cards.back: Watson conditioned a baby ('Little Albert') to fear a white rat by pairing it with a loud noise, demonstrating learned fear responses

cards.front: Albert Bandura — what is he known for?
cards.back: The Bobo doll experiment and social learning theory

cards.front: Who conducted the Bobo doll experiment on observational learning?
cards.back: Albert Bandura

cards.front: What did the Bobo doll experiment demonstrate?
cards.back: Children who watched adults act aggressively toward a Bobo doll imitated the aggressive behavior, supporting social learning theory

cards.front: What is social learning theory?
cards.back: Bandura's theory that people learn behaviors by observing and imitating others (modeling)

cards.front: Harry Harlow — what is he known for?
cards.back: Monkey attachment experiments showing that infant monkeys preferred a soft 'cloth mother' over a wire mother with food

cards.front: Who conducted experiments with infant monkeys and surrogate mothers to study attachment?
cards.back: Harry Harlow

cards.front: What did Harlow's monkey experiments demonstrate?
cards.back: That comfort and contact (attachment) are more important to infant development than food alone

cards.front: Solomon Asch — what is he known for?
cards.back: The Asch conformity experiments (line-length studies showing people conform to group pressure)

cards.front: Who conducted the famous conformity experiments using line-length comparisons?
cards.back: Solomon Asch

cards.front: What did the Asch conformity experiments demonstrate?
cards.back: That individuals will conform to an obviously incorrect group answer about one-third of the time due to social pressure

cards.front: Hermann Rorschach — what is he known for?
cards.back: The Rorschach inkblot test, a projective personality assessment

cards.front: Who created the inkblot test used in personality assessment?
cards.back: Hermann Rorschach

cards.front: Wilhelm Wundt — what is he known for?
cards.back: Establishing the first psychology laboratory in Leipzig, Germany (1879), earning him the title 'father of experimental psychology'

cards.front: Who established the first psychology laboratory in 1879?
cards.back: Wilhelm Wundt

cards.front: William James — what is he known for?
cards.back: Functionalism and writing 'The Principles of Psychology' (1890); often called the father of American psychology

cards.front: Who is considered the father of American psychology and founded the school of functionalism?
cards.back: William James

cards.front: What is the nature vs. nurture debate?
cards.back: The ongoing discussion of whether human behavior is determined more by genetics (nature) or environment and experience (nurture)

cards.front: What is cognitive dissonance?
cards.back: The mental discomfort experienced when holding two contradictory beliefs or when behavior conflicts with beliefs

cards.front: Who introduced the theory of cognitive dissonance in 1957?
cards.back: Leon Festinger

cards.front: What is confirmation bias?
cards.back: The tendency to search for, interpret, and remember information that confirms one's preexisting beliefs

cards.front: What is the placebo effect?
cards.back: A phenomenon in which a patient experiences real improvement after receiving an inactive treatment, due to their expectation of benefit

cards.front: What is the Hawthorne effect?
cards.back: The tendency for people to change their behavior when they know they are being observed or studied

cards.front: What school of psychology did John B. Watson found?
cards.back: Behaviorism — the view that psychology should study only observable behavior, not internal mental states

cards.front: What school of psychology did Wilhelm Wundt pioneer?
cards.back: Structuralism — using introspection to analyze the basic elements of conscious experience

cards.front: What school of psychology did William James pioneer?
cards.back: Functionalism — studying the purpose and function of mental processes rather than their structure
